ops.title: darwin FFI: dlsym data symbols and the unsafeptr lockstep
ops.body:
# The dlsym data-symbol pattern (trust/verify_darwin.go only)

CF-object DATA symbols (kSecGuestAttributeAudit, kSecCodeInfoStatus, kSecCodeInfoEntitlementsDict, kSecCodeInfoEntitlements, kCFBooleanFalse) are values at their dlsym address: dlsym returns the symbol's address, which must be DEREFERENCED to get the CFStringRef/CFBooleanRef. The dictionary callbacks (kCFTypeDictionaryKeyCallBacks/ValueCallBacks) are STRUCTs: the dlsym address is passed directly. The dereferenced address is a fixed dyld location, not a Go pointer — legitimate FFI that govet's unsafeptr heuristic cannot tell from a bug.

purego.RegisterLibFunc panics on a missing symbol, so symbols are probed with Dlsym first and framework skew fails closed through secErr instead of crashing.

# The three-config lockstep

`govet: disable: [unsafeptr]` in .golangci.yml is kept in lockstep with the `-unsafeptr=false` vet flag in Taskfile.yml and .github/workflows/ci.yml. Change one, change all three.

# Corrected attribution (fixes a .golangci.yml comment error)

Only `trust/verify_darwin.go` uses the raw Dlsym + unsafe.Pointer-deref pattern (~5 call sites). `appgroup/` does NOT — it uses purego's higher-level Objective-C messaging (objc.Send via stringWithUTF8String:/containerURL...); its only memory subtleties are that purego forwards Go string bytes uncopied (hence the trailing NUL) and that returned path/UTF8String pointers are autoreleased/interior, copied into Go memory during the send so no autorelease pool is needed. Whether appgroup/ still trips unsafeptr at all is unverified — see the backlog task about narrowing the disable.

# Related G103 suppressions

wire/peer_darwin.go's getsockopt needs raw buffer/len pointers; purego.SyscallN is go:uintptrescapes so the args stay pinned across the call. trust/verify_darwin.go's `*(*uintptr)(unsafe.Pointer(sym))` dereferences a fixed dlsym data-symbol address.
